perturbation.original_text: NIST shall keep the information confidential and shall use the Proprietary Information only for internal experimental purposes or evaluation of the Proprietary Information. NIST shall not make any copies of the Proprietary Information except as necessary for its employees who are entitled to review it under Section 1 above. Any copies which are made shall be identified as belonging to Discloser and marked as “confidential .”
perturbation.changed_text: NIST shall keep the information confidential and shall use the Proprietary Information only for internal experimental purposes or evaluation of the Proprietary Information. NIST may make copies of the Proprietary Information at its discretion, even if not strictly necessary for its employees to review it under Section 1 above. Copies made do not need to be identified as belonging to Discloser or marked as “confidential.”
perturbation.explanation: Modifying this section allows NIST to make unlimited copies without confidentiality restrictions, directly contradicting the intent of protecting proprietary information. This introduces ambiguity and undermines the NDA's purpose.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1905 - Disclosure of confidential information generally
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially violates 18 U.S. Code § 1905 because it removes the requirement to keep the information confidential and allows for unrestricted copying, creating a risk of unauthorized disclosure of proprietary information that could affect competitive standing.

Disclosure of confidential information generally Whoever, being an officer or employee of the United States or of any department or agency thereof, any person acting on behalf of the Federal Housing Finance Agency, or agent of the Department of Justice as defined in the Antitrust Civil Process Act ( 15 U.S.C. 1311–1314 ), or being an employee of a private sector org
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the intent of protecting proprietary information, creating a substantial risk of unauthorized disclosure. The original text had specific restrictions on copying and mandated confidentiality markings. The changed text removes these restrictions, allowing unrestricted copying and eliminating the confidentiality requirement. This conflicts with the Law Explanation stating that the changes potentially violate 18 U.S. Code § 1905 because they remove the requirement to keep the information confidential and allow for unrestricted copying. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1905, concerns the 'Disclosure of confidential information generally,' which the modified text directly undermines.

perturbation.original_text: To the extent permitted by law, NIST shall prot ect the disclosed Proprietary Information by using the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, to prevent the unauthorized use, dissemination or publication of the Proprietary Information as NIST uses to protect its own confident ial information of a like nature.
perturbation.changed_text: NIST shall protect the disclosed Proprietary Information only to the extent it deems necessary, using its own discretion regarding the degree of care required to prevent unauthorized use, dissemination, or publication. This standard may be lower than the care NIST uses for its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the standard of care NIST must exercise, making it discretionary and potentially inadequate, conflicting with standard practices for protecting proprietary information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 552a - Records maintained on individuals
perturbation.law_explanation: While seemingly related to privacy, this change can indirectly contradict 5 U.S. Code § 552a if the 'Proprietary Information' contains personal information. By lowering the standard of care, NIST could potentially violate the Act's requirement to maintain information with accuracy, relevance, timeliness, and completeness, especially if it leads to unauthorized disclosure.

Records maintained on individuals (a) Definitions .-For purposes of this section- (1) the term "agency" means agency as defined in section 552(e) 1 of this title; (2) the term "individual" means a citizen of the United States or an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence; (3) the term "maintain" includes maintain, collect, use, or disseminate; (4) the term "record" means any item, collection, or grouping of information about an individual that is maintained by an agency, including, but not limited to, his education, financial transactions, medical history, and criminal or employment history and that contains his name, or the identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual, such as a finger or voice print or a photograph; (5) the term "system of records" means a group of any records under the control of any agency from which information is retrieved by the name of the individual or by some identifying number, symbol, or other identifying particular assigned to the individual; (6) the term "statistical record" means a record in a system of records maintained for statistical research or reporting purposes only and not used in whole or in part in making any determination about an identifiable individual, except as provided by section 8 of title 13 ; (7) the term "routine use" means, with respect to the disclosure of a record, the use of such record for a purpose which is compatible with the purpose for which it was collected; (8) the term "matching program"- (A) means any computerized comparison of- (i) two or more automated systems of records or a system of records with non-Federal records for the purpose of- (I) establishing or verifying the eligibility of, or continuing compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements by, applicants for, recipients or beneficiaries of, participants in, or providers of services with respect to, cash or in-kind assistance or payments under Federal benefit programs, or (II) recouping payments or delinquent debts under such Federal benefit programs, or (ii) two or more automated Federal personnel or payroll systems of records
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by significantly weakening the standard of care NIST must exercise in protecting proprietary information. While the original text mandates 'the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care,' the modified text grants NIST discretion, potentially allowing a lower standard than used for its own confidential information. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with 5 U.S. Code § 552a if the proprietary information includes personal information, as the reduced standard of care could compromise the accuracy, relevance, timeliness, and completeness of the information, potentially leading to unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'record' as 'any item, collection, or grouping of information about an individual that is maintained by an agency...' If the Proprietary Information falls under this definition, the weakening of the standard of care directly undermines the protections intended by the law, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: NIST shall only have a duty to protect the Proprietary Information which is disclosed to it in writing and is identified as “confidential ” by Discloser, or, if disclosed orally or in any other manner, if Discloser provides NIST with a written memorandum summarizing the Proprietary Information and designating such summary as “confidential ” within thirty (30) days of the disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: NIST's duty to protect the Proprietary Information applies only to information disclosed in writing and identified as "confidential" by the Discloser. Oral disclosures or information provided in any other manner are not subject to protection, regardless of whether a written memorandum is provided.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the provision for protecting orally disclosed information when accompanied by a written summary, the contract narrows the scope of protection and may contradict established legal norms for protecting trade secrets, which can be disclosed orally.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the spirit and potentially the letter of 18 U.S. Code § 1839, which defines trade secrets. The definition doesn't specify that trade secrets must be disclosed in writing to be protected. By excluding oral disclosures from protection, the modified text creates a loophole where valuable, orally disclosed trade secrets would not be covered by the NDA, conflicting with the intent of protecting proprietary information.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit of trade secret protection as outlined in 18 U.S. Code § 1839, because it limits protection only to written disclosures, whereas the law does not specify that trade secrets must be disclosed in writing. The original contract provided a mechanism to protect orally disclosed information, while the changed text eliminates this protection entirely. The scraped snippet 1 defines terms used in the chapter on protection of trade secrets, and does not specify that information has to be disclosed in writing to be covered.

perturbation.original_text: These provisions are consistent with and do not supersede, conflict with, or otherwise alter the employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by existing statute or Executive order relating to NIST EMPLOYEE -- Please route this NDA with an NDA Memo for review and signatures. When fully signed, please forward a copy of Memo and NDA to the Technology Partnerships Office (TPO) at Mail Stop 2200. Please contact TPO if you have questio ns at (301) 975 -2573.
perturbation.changed_text: These provisions supersede any conflicting employee obligations, rights, or liabilities created by existing statute or Executive order relating to NIST EMPLOYEE. Please route this NDA with an NDA Memo for review and signatures. When fully signed, please forward a copy of Memo and NDA to the Technology Partnerships Office (TPO) at Mail Stop 2200. Please contact TPO if you have questio ns at (301) 975 -2573.
perturbation.explanation: This modification states that the NDA supersedes existing employee obligations, potentially conflicting with whistleblower protection laws and other statutes that protect federal employees. This creates a legal conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 5 U.S. Code § 7211 - Employees' right to petition Congress
perturbation.law_explanation: This change directly contradicts 5 U.S. Code § 7211, which guarantees federal employees the right to petition Congress. By stating that the NDA supersedes existing employee obligations, the modified text could be interpreted as restricting an employee's ability to communicate with Congress about issues related to the disclosed information, thereby violating their statutory right.

Employees' right to petition Congress The right of employees, individually or collectively, to petition Congress or a Member of Congress, or to furnish information to either House of Congress, or to a committee or Member thereof, may not be int
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 5 U.S. Code § 7211. The original text states the NDA does not supersede employee obligations, while the changed text states that it does. This conflicts with the right of employees to furnish information to Congress, as guaranteed by 5 USC 7211: 'The right of employees, individually or collectively, to petition Congress or a Member of Congress, or to furnish information to either House of Congress, or to a committee or Member thereof, may not be int-'. The changed text could restrict an employee's ability to communicate with Congress, directly violating their statutory right.
